5	
6	For the full auto-generated reference — one page per command with all flags and
7	subcommands — see the [Command Reference](/docs/cli/commands/).
8	
9	## Quick Reference
10	
11	### Setup
12	
13	```bash
14	ddx init              # Initialize DDx in your project
15	ddx install helix     # Install a workflow plugin
16	ddx doctor            # Check installation health
17	ddx upgrade           # Upgrade DDx binary
18	```
19	
20	### Beads (Work Tracker)
21	
22	The bead tracker is the core of DDx. Beads are work items with dependencies,
23	claims, and status. Workflow tools like HELIX use beads to drive execution.
24	
25	```bash
26	ddx bead create "Title" --type task
27	ddx bead list
28	ddx bead show <id>
29	ddx bead ready              # unblocked beads
30	ddx bead blocked            # beads waiting on deps
31	ddx bead update <id> --claim
32	ddx bead close <id>
33	ddx bead dep add <id> <dep>
34	ddx bead dep tree <id>
35	```
36	
37	### Execution Engine
38	
39	Define reusable execution definitions and run them with recorded evidence.
40	
41	```bash
42	ddx exec define <name> --artifact <id> --command "go test ./..."
43	ddx exec run <id>
44	ddx exec list
45	ddx exec history --artifact <id>
46	ddx exec result <run-id>
47	ddx exec log <run-id>
48	```
49	
50	### Agent Dispatch
51	
52	```bash
53	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt file.md
54	ddx agent run --quorum majority --harnesses codex,claude --text "Review this"
55	ddx agent list
56	ddx agent usage
57	ddx agent capabilities claude
58	```
59	
60	### Package Registry
61	
62	```bash
63	ddx search <query>
64	ddx install <name>
65	ddx installed
66	ddx uninstall <name>
67	```
68	
69	### Documents
70	
71	```bash
72	ddx doc history <id>
73	ddx doc changed --since HEAD~5
74	ddx checkpoint <name>
75	ddx list
76	```
77	
78	### Configuration
79	
80	```yaml
81	# .ddx/config.yaml
82	agent:
83	  harness: claude
84	  permissions: safe         # safe | supervised | unrestricted
85	git:
86	  auto_commit: never        # always | prompt | never
87	```
88	
89	### Global Flags
90	
91	| Flag | Description |
92	|------|------------|
93	| `-v` | Verbose output |
94	| `--config` | Config file path |
95	| `--help` | Show help |

6	## ddx agent
7	
8	Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging
9	
10	### Synopsis
11	
12	Unified interface for dispatching work to AI coding agents.
13	
14	Supports multiple harnesses (codex, claude, gemini, etc.) with output capture,
15	token tracking, session logging, and multi-agent quorum.
16	
17	The embedded DDx agent harness is named 'agent' and is always available without
18	installing external binaries. Use --harness agent or --profile cheap to route to it.
19	
20	Profile routing (--profile default|cheap|fast|smart) selects the best available harness
21	and model automatically. Workflow tools should prefer --profile over --harness to
22	stay decoupled from harness installation details.
23	
24	Examples:
25	  ddx agent run --profile default --prompt task.md
26	  ddx agent run --profile cheap --prompt task.md
27	  ddx agent run --profile smart --prompt task.md
28	  ddx agent run --profile smart --model gpt-5.4   # explicit override; avoid by default
29	  ddx agent run --harness agent --prompt task.md
30	  ddx agent run --harness codex --prompt task.md
31	  ddx agent run --quorum majority --harnesses codex,claude --prompt task.md
32	  ddx agent list
33	  ddx agent capabilities agent
34	  ddx agent capabilities codex
35	  ddx agent doctor
36	  ddx agent log
37	
38	```
39	ddx agent [flags]
40	```
41	
42	### Options
43	
44	```
45	  -h, --help   help for agent
46	```

48	### Options inherited from parent commands

54	```

5	
6	## ddx work
7	
8	Work the bead execution queue
9	
10	### Synopsis
11	
12	work is the primary operator-facing surface for draining the bead
13	execution queue. It is an alias for "ddx agent execute-loop" — all flags
14	and behavior are identical.
15	
16	execute-loop is the primary queue-driven execution surface. It scans the
17	target project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next ready bead,
18	runs "ddx agent execute-bead" on it from the project root, records the
19	structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.
20	
21	Reach for execute-loop by default. Use "ddx agent execute-bead" directly
22	only as the primitive for debugging or re-running one specific bead.
23	
24	Planning and document-only beads are valid execution targets — any bead
25	with unmet acceptance criteria and no blocking deps is eligible.
26	
27	Close semantics (per execute-bead result status):
28	  success                      — close bead with session + commit evidence
29	  already_satisfied            — close bead (after repeated no_changes)
30	  no_changes                   — unclaim; may cooldown or close after retries
31	  land_conflict                — unclaim; result preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/
32	  post_run_check_failed        — unclaim; result preserved
33	  execution_failed             — unclaim
34	  structural_validation_failed — unclaim
35	
36	Only success (and already_satisfied) closes the bead. Every other status
37	leaves the bead open and unclaimed so a later attempt can try again. Each
38	attempt is appended to the bead as an execute-bead event (status, detail,
39	base_rev, result_rev, preserve_ref, retry_after), and the underlying agent
40	session log is recorded under the execute-bead agent-log path.
41	
42	By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background
43	worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current
44	process.
45	
46	Project targeting (multi-project servers):
47	  --project <path>    target a specific project root (absolute path or name)
48	  DDX_PROJECT_ROOT    env var fallback; used when --project is not set
49	  (default)           the git root of the current working directory
50	
51	When submitting to a multi-project server you must ensure the target project
52	is registered with the server (run "ddx server" from that directory, or use
53	"ddx server projects register"). The server rejects unrecognised project paths.
54	
55	
56	```
57	ddx work [flags]
58	```
59	
60	### Examples
61	
62	```
63	  # Drain the current execution-ready queue once and exit
64	  ddx work
65	
66	  # Pick one ready bead, execute it, and stop
67	  ddx work --profile default --once
68	
69	  # Run continuously as a bounded queue worker
70	  ddx work --poll-interval 30s
71	
72	  # Force a specific harness/model for a debugging pass
73	  ddx work --once --harness agent --model minimax/minimax-m2.7
74	
75	  # Run inline in the current process
76	  ddx work --local --once
77	```
78	
79	### Options
80	
81	```
82	      --adaptive-min-tier-window int   Trailing window size for adaptive min-tier evaluation (default 50)
83	      --effort string                  Effort level
84	      --from string                    Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)
85	      --harness string                 Agent harness to use
86	  -h, --help                           help for work
87	      --json                           Output loop result as JSON
88	      --local                          Run inline in current process instead of server worker (default: submit to server)
89	      --max-cost float                 Stop the loop when accumulated billed cost exceeds USD; 0 = unlimited; subscription and local providers do not count (default 100)
90	      --max-tier string                Maximum tier for auto-escalation: cheap, standard, or smart (default: smart)
91	      --min-tier string                Minimum tier for auto-escalation: cheap, standard, or smart (default: cheap)
92	      --model string                   Model override
93	      --model-ref string               Model catalog reference (e.g. code-medium); resolved via the model catalog
94	      --no-adaptive-min-tier           Disable adaptive min-tier promotion based on trailing cheap-tier success rate
95	      --no-review                      Skip post-merge review (e.g. for doc-only beads or tight iteration loops)
96	      --once                           Process at most one ready bead
97	      --poll-interval duration         Poll interval for continuous scanning; zero drains current ready work and exits
98	      --profile string                 Routing profile: default, cheap, fast, or smart (default "default")
99	      --project string                 Target project root path or name (default: CWD git root). Env: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT
100	      --provider string                Provider name (e.g. vidar, openrouter); selects a named provider from config
101	      --review-harness string          Harness to use for the post-merge reviewer (default: same as implementation harness)
102	      --review-model string            Model override for the post-merge reviewer (default: smart tier)
103	```
104	
105	### Options inherited from parent commands
106	
107	```
108	      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
109	      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
110	  -v, --verbose                    verbose output
111	```
112	
113	### SEE ALSO
114	
115	* [ddx](/docs/cli/commands/ddx/)	 - Document-Driven Development eXperience - AI development toolkit

1	---
2	title: DDx Skills
3	weight: 7
4	---
5	
6	DDx ships 6 built-in skills that agents discover as slash commands. These
7	guide agents through DDx operations instead of requiring them to memorize
8	flag combinations.
9	
10	## Available Skills
11	
12	| Skill | Description |
13	|-------|-------------|
14	| `/ddx-bead` | Create and manage beads with proper metadata |
15	| `/ddx-agent` | Select harness, model, and effort for agent dispatch |
16	| `/ddx-install` | Search, preview, and install packages |
17	| `/ddx-status` | Project health overview |
18	| `/ddx-review` | Multi-agent quorum and fresh-eyes code review |
19	| `/ddx-run` | Execute a bead end-to-end: claim → build → verify → close |
20	
21	Skills are installed automatically by `ddx init` to `~/.agents/skills/ddx-*/`.
22	
23	## `/ddx-bead` — Create Work Items
24	
25	Instead of remembering flags, invoke the skill:
26	
27	```
28	/ddx-bead "Implement user login"
29	```
30	
31	The skill guides the agent through:
32	- Choosing type (task, epic, bug, chore)
33	- Setting labels and spec-id
34	- Writing acceptance criteria
35	- Wiring dependencies
36	
37	## `/ddx-review` — Multi-Agent Review
38	
39	Run a structured code review across multiple harnesses:
40	
41	```
42	/ddx-review
43	```
44	
45	The skill:
46	1. Checks available harnesses via `ddx agent list`
47	2. Assembles a review prompt with relevant context
48	3. Dispatches via `ddx agent run --quorum majority --harnesses codex,claude`
49	4. Reports where models agree and disagree
50	
51	### Example: Review a bead's implementation
52	
53	```
54	/ddx-review bead ddx-abc123
55	```
56	
57	The skill reads the bead's spec-id and acceptance criteria, then reviews
58	the implementation against them.
59	
60	## `/ddx-run` — Execute a Bead
61	
62	The full lifecycle for implementing a work item:
63	
64	```
65	/ddx-run ddx-abc123
66	```
67	
68	The skill:
69	1. Reads the bead's spec-id and acceptance criteria
70	2. Loads the governing artifact
71	3. Claims the bead (`ddx bead update <id> --claim`)
72	4. Dispatches an agent with full context
73	5. Verifies tests pass after implementation
74	6. Closes the bead if acceptance criteria are met
75	
76	This prevents agents from skipping verification or claiming completion
77	without running tests.
78	
79	## `/ddx-agent` — Guided Dispatch
80	
81	When you need to run an agent but aren't sure which model or effort level:
82	
83	```
84	/ddx-agent
85	```
86	
87	Shows available harnesses, their capabilities, and helps select the right
88	configuration before dispatching.
89	
90	## `/ddx-status` — Health Check
91	
92	Quick overview of project state:
93	
94	```
95	/ddx-status
96	```
97	
98	Runs `ddx doctor`, `ddx bead list`, and `ddx bead ready` and summarizes
99	the findings.
100	
101	## Creating Custom Skills
102	
103	See [Creating Plugins](../plugins) for how to add your own skills to DDx
104	or distribute them as a plugin.
105

167		require.NoError(t, err)
168		content := string(data)

170		// User content outside markers must survive
171		assert.Contains(t, content, "User content before the DDx block.", "pre-block user content lost")
172		assert.Contains(t, content, "User content after the DDx block.", "post-block user content lost")
173		// New DDx block content must be present
174		assert.Contains(t, content, "This project uses [DDx]", "new DDx block content not injected")

176		assert.Equal(t, 1, countOccurrences(content, agentsMarkerStart), "should have exactly one start marker")

180		generateAgentsMD(workingDir)
181		data2, err := os.ReadFile(agentsPath)
182		require.NoError(t, err)
183		content2 := string(data2)
184		assert.Equal(t, 1, countOccurrences(content2, agentsMarkerStart), "re-run duplicated start marker")
185		assert.Equal(t, 1, countOccurrences(content2, agentsMarkerEnd), "re-run duplicated end marker")

222	// countOccurrences is a small test helper; we avoid strings.Count import noise here.
223	func countOccurrences(s, sub string) int {
224		count := 0
225		start := 0
226		for {
227			idx := indexFrom(s, sub, start)
228			if idx == -1 {
229				return count
230			}
231			count++
232			start = idx + len(sub)
233		}
234	}
235	
236	func indexFrom(s, sub string, start int) int {
237		if start > len(s) {
238			return -1
239		}
240		rest := s[start:]
241		for i := 0; i+len(sub) <= len(rest); i++ {
242			if rest[i:i+len(sub)] == sub {
243				return start + i
244			}
245		}
246		return -1
247	}

342					assert.NoError(t, err)

344

348			})
349		}

6	## ddx work
7	
8	Drain the bead execution queue
9	
10	### Synopsis
11	
12	work drains the execution-ready bead queue. It is the FEAT-010 layer-3
13	queue drain: it iterates ddx try (layer 2) across ready beads until a stop
14	condition is met and owns retry-power policy between attempts.
15	
16	Unlike "ddx agent execute-loop", ddx work treats --harness, --provider, and
17	--model as opaque passthrough constraints forwarded to the agent unchanged.
18	DDx does not validate these values or branch on them; the agent owns routing
19	within the requested power bounds.
20	
21	Stop conditions (evaluated between attempts):
22	  drained     — no ready beads remain
23	  blocked     — every remaining bead has produced a terminal non-success outcome
24	  deferred    — configured budget exhausted
25	  no_progress — N consecutive attempts produced no commit (default N=3)
26	  signal      — SIGINT/SIGTERM received between attempts
27	
28	work runs inline in the current process; per ADR-022 there is no separate
29	"submit to server" mode. The legacy --local flag is accepted but ignored
30	(deprecation warning printed) and will be removed in a future release.
31	
32	
33	```
34	ddx work [flags]
35	```
36	
37	### Examples
38	
39	```
40	  # Drain the current execution-ready queue once and exit
41	  ddx work
42	
43	  # Pick one ready bead, execute it, and stop
44	  ddx work --once
45	
46	  # Run continuously as a bounded queue worker
47	  ddx work --poll-interval 30s
48	
49	  # Forward harness/model as passthrough constraints (ddx does not validate these)
50	  ddx work --once --harness agent --model minimax/minimax-m2.7
51	
52	  # Constrain power tier (retry-power policy is owned by ddx work)
53	  ddx work --once --min-power 40 --max-power 90
54	```
55	
56	### Options
57	
58	```
59	      --effort string              Effort level
60	      --from string                Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)
61	      --harness string             Agent harness constraint (passthrough; ddx work does not validate)
62	  -h, --help                       help for work
63	      --json                       Output loop result as JSON
64	      --max-cost float             Stop when accumulated billed cost exceeds USD; 0 = unlimited (default 100)
65	      --max-power int              Maximum model power allowed (0 = unconstrained); passed to agent routing unchanged
66	      --min-power int              Minimum model power required (0 = unconstrained); passed to agent routing unchanged
67	      --model string               Model constraint (passthrough; ddx work does not validate)
68	      --model-ref string           Model catalog reference (e.g. code-medium); resolved via the model catalog
69	      --no-review                  Skip post-merge review
70	      --once                       Process at most one ready bead
71	      --poll-interval duration     Poll interval for continuous scanning; zero drains current ready work and exits (legacy opt-out). Default 30s keeps the worker alive across empty polls. (default 30s)

> work is the primary operator-facing surface for draining the bead
> execution queue. It is an alias for "ddx agent execute-loop" — all flags
> and behavior are identical.
> 
> execute-loop is the primary queue-driven execution surface. It scans the
> target project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next ready bead,
> runs "ddx agent execute-bead" on it from the project root, records the
> structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.
> 
> Reach for execute-loop by default. Use "ddx agent execute-bead" directly
> only as the primitive for debugging or re-running one specific bead.
> 
> Planning and document-only beads are valid execution targets — any bead
> with unmet acceptance criteria and no blocking deps is eligible.
> 
> Close semantics (per execute-bead result status):
>   success                      — close bead with session + commit evidence
>   already_satisfied            — close bead (after repeated no_changes)
>   no_changes                   — unclaim; may cooldown or close after retries
>   land_conflict                — unclaim; result preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/
>   post_run_check_failed        — unclaim; result preserved
>   execution_failed             — unclaim
>   structural_validation_failed — unclaim
> 
> Only success (and already_satisfied) closes the bead. Every other status
> leaves the bead open and unclaimed so a later attempt can try again. Each
> attempt is appended to the bead as an execute-bead event (status, detail,
> base_rev, result_rev, preserve_ref, retry_after), and the underlying agent
> session log is recorded under the execute-bead agent-log path.
> 
> By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background
> worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current
> process.

> Project targeting (multi-project servers):
>   --project <path>    target a specific project root (absolute path or name)
>   DDX_PROJECT_ROOT    env var fallback; used when --project is not set

> 
> When submitting to a multi-project server you must ensure the target project
> is registered with the server (run "ddx server" from that directory, or use
> "ddx server projects register"). The server rejects unrecognised project paths.

# DDx

DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
orchestrates work while Fizeau owns provider/model routing, alias
resolution, fuzzy matching of raw model strings, catalog lookups, and
transcript/session rendering), a persona system (bindable AI
personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.

## How this skill works

The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.

**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
guidance, not this overview alone.**

## Vocabulary

Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
exact definitions.

- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
  `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
  passthrough constraints.
- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
  finalization.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
  branch on harness names.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
  orchestration; that remains harness business.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.

## Links out

- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
  executions, and skills features.
- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
- Open standard this skill conforms to:
  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
  `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
  passthrough constraints.
- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
  finalization.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
  branch on harness names.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
  orchestration; that remains harness business.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.

## Links out

- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
  executions, and skills features.
- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
- Open standard this skill conforms to:
  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

input.old_string:
## Queue Work

Drain the bead execution queue. `ddx work` is an alias for
`ddx agent execute-loop`; all flags pass through.

```bash
ddx work                    # drain the bead execution queue
```

## Agent Service

Invoke AI agents against prompts, run multi-agent consensus, or execute a
bead end-to-end in an isolated worktree.

```bash
ddx agent run --harness=<name> --prompt <file>
ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=a,b
ddx agent execute-bead <id> [--from <rev>] [--no-merge]
ddx agent execute-loop          # drain bead queue (prefer `ddx work`)
ddx agent list                  # available harnesses
ddx agent doctor                # harness health check
ddx agent log                   # session history
```
input.new_string:
## Queue Work

Primary execution surface for draining the bead queue end-to-end.

```bash
ddx work                    # drain the bead execution queue
ddx try <id>                # attempt one bead in an isolated worktree
ddx run --prompt <file>     # invoke the agent once
```

## Agent Diagnostics

Inspect harness health, session logs, routing, and provider availability.
The `ddx agent` namespace is a diagnostics surface; use `ddx run`, `ddx try`,
and `ddx work` for execution.

```bash
ddx agent list              # available harnesses
ddx agent doctor            # harness health check
ddx agent log               # session history
ddx agent capabilities      # model and reasoning-level capabilities
ddx agent providers         # configured providers with live status
```

## ddx agent execute-loop

Drain the single-project execution-ready bead queue

### Synopsis

execute-loop is the primary queue-driven execution surface. It scans the
target project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next ready bead,
runs "ddx agent execute-bead" on it from the project root, records the
structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.

Reach for execute-loop by default. Use "ddx agent execute-bead" directly
only as the primitive for debugging or re-running one specific bead.

Planning and document-only beads are valid execution targets — any bead
with unmet acceptance criteria and no blocking deps is eligible.

Close semantics (per execute-bead result status):
  success                      — close bead with session + commit evidence
  already_satisfied            — close bead (after repeated no_changes)

## ddx agent execute-bead

Removed compatibility surface.

Use `ddx try <bead-id>` for one bead attempt. Use `ddx work` for normal queue
draining. `ddx try` owns bead attempt setup and result classification, then
delegates the actual agent invocation to the simple `ddx run` layer.

Harness, provider, and model values may be passed through for explicit operator
debugging, but DDx should otherwise request an agent power range and let the
agent choose the concrete model/provider route.

### Options inherited from parent commands

```
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output
```
